Course Title: Who Is Responsible for AI Decisions in Public Services?
Category: Education & Public Sector: Education, Government Services, Non-Profit organizations
Description: Accountability across vendors, agencies, and policymakers.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with how public services operate and a general awareness of artificial intelligence as a technology are all that is needed to get started with this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key stakeholders responsible for AI decisions in public services and explain how accountability is shared across vendors, government agencies, and policymakers. You will also apply practical frameworks to recognise where responsibility lies when AI systems affect citizens, helping you navigate real-world scenarios with greater confidence and clarity.

lessons.lesson title: Who Is Responsible for AI Decisions in Public Services?
lessons.Description: When artificial intelligence is used to make decisions in public services, such as allocating benefits, triaging healthcare, or flagging individuals for investigation, it is not always clear who should be held accountable when something goes wrong. This lesson explores the shared and sometimes competing responsibilities of the technology vendors who build AI systems, the public agencies that deploy them, and the policymakers who set the rules governing their use. We will look at real-world examples to understand how accountability can become fragmented, leaving citizens without clear routes to challenge decisions that affect their lives. By the end of this lesson, you will be able to identify the key parties involved in AI decision-making in public services and understand the basic principles that should guide accountability. No prior technical knowledge is required, and the focus throughout is on practical understanding rather than complex theory.
lessons.content: Accountability across vendors, agencies, and policymakers.

slides.subtitle: Tracing fault lines in public AI.
slides.slidecontent:
• AI systems in public services making decisions that affect real people's lives
• When something goes wrong, blame is rarely straightforward
• Multiple parties involved: vendors, government agencies, and elected officials
• Fault lines often hidden inside contracts and technical systems
• No single rulebook yet exists
• This lesson maps where responsibility actually sits

slides.subtitle: When responsibility falls through the cracks
slides.slidecontent:
• No clear owner of AI-driven decisions
• Responsibility falls between organisations
• Vendors point to agencies, agencies point to policy
• Citizens left without clear routes for redress
• Damage often done before anyone takes ownership
• Understanding gaps is key to fixing them

slides.subtitle: From vendors to citizens receiving decisions
slides.slidecontent:
• Vendors build and train the AI system
• Agencies choose, deploy, and configure the tool
• Managers oversee system performance and outputs
• Frontline staff act on AI recommendations
• Citizens experience real-world consequences
• Each link connects to the next

slides.subtitle: What technology companies must deliver
slides.slidecontent:
• Build systems that perform as documented
• Conduct thorough testing before release
• Clearly disclose limitations and known biases
• Provide ongoing technical support
• Update systems when problems are identified
• Maintain transparent documentation throughout

slides.subtitle: How public services must govern AI deployment
slides.slidecontent:
• Verify AI tools meet legal and ethical standards
• Configure systems appropriately for local context
• Train staff on proper use and limitations
• Monitor outcomes for fairness and accuracy
• Establish clear escalation procedures
• Maintain human oversight of all decisions

slides.subtitle: Setting the legal and ethical framework
slides.slidecontent:
• Define legal boundaries for AI use in public services
• Establish accountability requirements for all parties
• Create citizen rights and appeal mechanisms
• Set data protection and privacy standards
• Enable regulatory oversight and enforcement
• Update laws as technology evolves

slides.subtitle: Preventing gaps through clear agreements
slides.slidecontent:
• Each party must understand their specific role
• Clear contracts define vendor obligations
• Agencies document deployment decisions and oversight
• Regular communication between all stakeholders
• Joint responsibility for monitoring outcomes
• Transparent processes for addressing failures

slides.subtitle: Applying these principles in practice
slides.slidecontent:
• Identify AI systems already in use around you
• Ask questions about documentation and oversight
• Check if human decision-makers remain in control
• Look for clear accountability agreements
• Advocate for regular system reviews
• Start building better AI governance today

scripts.script: When an AI system makes a decision that affects someone's benefits, housing application, or access to a public service, and that decision turns out to be wrong, who is actually responsible? That question sounds simple, but in practice it is anything but. You might have a private technology company that built the system, a government agency that deployed it, and elected officials who approved the whole thing. Each one can point to someone else. And the real fault lines, the places where accountability breaks down, are often buried deep inside contracts or wrapped up in technical processes that most people never see. There is no single, clear rulebook that tells us how to handle this yet. So what this lesson sets out to do is map the landscape honestly. We will look at where responsibility actually sits, who tends to carry the burden when things go wrong, and why that picture is so much more complicated than it should be.

So let's think about what actually happens when an AI system makes a poor decision in a public service. The first question everyone asks is: who is responsible? And more often than not, the honest answer is that nobody is entirely sure. You might have a technology vendor who built the system, a government agency that commissioned it, and a frontline team that uses it every day. Each of those parties has a role, but none of them fully owns the outcome. What tends to happen is that accountability falls into the gaps between those organisations, and it just sits there, unaddressed. When something goes wrong, responsibility gets passed around. The vendor points to the agency, the agency points to the policy, and the people affected are left waiting for answers. And by the time anyone does step forward to take responsibility, the damage is already done.

To understand accountability in AI-powered public services, we need to see it as a chain connecting multiple parties. It starts with technology vendors who build and train the AI systems. Then you have public agencies that choose to adopt these tools and decide how to deploy them in their specific context. Managers sit in the middle, responsible for overseeing how well the system performs and what kinds of decisions it's producing. Frontline staff receive AI-generated recommendations and must act on them, often under significant time pressure. And at the end of this chain are citizens who experience the real-world consequences, whether that's a benefit being approved, a referral being made, or support being denied. Each link in this chain is connected to the next, and understanding these connections is essential for working out where accountability genuinely sits.

Let's start with the technology vendors, the companies that actually build AI systems for public use. Their accountability begins with delivering systems that genuinely perform as promised in their documentation. This means conducting thorough testing before any system goes live, being honest about limitations and potential biases, and providing the technical support needed to keep systems running properly. When problems are identified, vendors must be prepared to update and improve their systems. Most importantly, they need to maintain clear, accessible documentation throughout the entire lifecycle of their product. This isn't just about meeting contractual obligations, it's about ensuring that everyone else in the chain has the information they need to use the system responsibly.

Public agencies carry a different but equally important set of responsibilities. Before deploying any AI tool, they must verify that it meets both legal requirements and ethical standards for their specific context. This includes configuring the system appropriately, training staff on its proper use and limitations, and establishing clear procedures for when things go wrong. Perhaps most critically, agencies must maintain ongoing monitoring to ensure the AI system continues to produce fair and accurate outcomes over time. They also need to preserve human oversight, making sure that AI recommendations support human decision-makers rather than replace them entirely. The agency's role is to bridge between the technical capabilities the vendor provides and the real-world needs of citizens they serve.

Policymakers operate at a different level, setting the overall framework within which everyone else must work. Their job is to define the legal boundaries for how AI can be used in public services, establish clear accountability requirements for vendors and agencies, and create meaningful rights for citizens to challenge AI-influenced decisions. This includes setting robust data protection and privacy standards, enabling effective regulatory oversight, and ensuring enforcement mechanisms actually work when problems arise. Critically, policymakers must also be prepared to update laws and regulations as AI technology continues to evolve. Their decisions create the foundation that determines whether the entire system of AI accountability can function effectively.

So we've established that accountability must be shared, but how do we make that actually work in practice? The key is preventing those dangerous gaps where responsibility falls through the cracks. This starts with everyone understanding their specific role and having that clearly documented. Contracts between agencies and vendors need to spell out exactly what each party is responsible for. Agencies must document their own deployment decisions and oversight processes. All stakeholders need regular communication channels so problems can be identified and addressed quickly. Most importantly, there must be joint responsibility for monitoring how these systems affect real people over time. When failures occur, the focus should be on transparent processes for addressing them, not on shifting blame between parties.

Now it's time to put these principles into practice. Start by looking around you and identifying AI systems that are already being used in public services you encounter or work with. Ask questions about how they're documented, who's providing oversight, and whether human decision-makers are truly remaining in control. Look for evidence of clear accountability agreements between the different parties involved. Advocate for regular reviews of these systems to ensure they're still working as intended. The principles we've covered aren't complicated, but they do require active engagement from everyone involved. The goal isn't perfection, it's building systems where accountability is clear, problems can be addressed quickly, and citizens have genuine routes for redress when things go wrong.

Who is this for: Public administrators, government employees, policy analysts, civil servants, procurement officers, program managers, compliance officers, public sector managers
Team or Dept this is for: Government Affairs, Public Administration, Policy, Compliance, Legal, Procurement, Operations
